does: This paper develops and implements the first practical infra-Bayesian reinforcement learning agent that operates without assuming the environment is realizable or independent of the agent's policy. The core contribution is demonstrating that worst-case evaluation—a hallmark of infra-Bayesian decision theory—empirically outperforms classical RL methods in adversarial and non-realizable settings. The approach distinguishes between ordinary probabilistic uncertainty and Knightian uncertainty, evaluating actions based on their worst-case outcomes rather than posterior expectations. The implementation shows concrete advantages over standard Bayesian and classical RL approaches when environments are misspecified or contain other agents that anticipate the agent's behavior.
connects: This work bridges decision theory and reinforcement learning by applying infra-Bayesianism, a framework developed to handle model misspecification and Knightian uncertainty, to the RL setting. It addresses fundamental limitations of classical RL and Bayesian methods in non-realizable environments—problems highlighted in recent work on robust learning under distribution shift and adversarial settings. The paper responds to critiques of standard probabilistic approaches in multi-agent and human-AI interaction scenarios, where agents strategically respond to observed policies. It connects to safety-relevant problems where environments contain predictors and other agents, building on prior work examining how agent behavior can influence the world model itself.
enables: This work opens new directions for building robust RL agents in safety-critical domains involving humans, other AI systems, and strategic actors. It suggests infra-Bayesian methods could improve reliability in misspecified or adversarially-influenced environments without requiring accurate prior construction. Future work could extend this approach to larger state-action spaces, develop more efficient worst-case approximations, and test the framework in complex multi-agent and human-AI collaboration scenarios. The practical implementation demonstrates a pathway toward decision-making systems that gracefully degrade under model failure rather than producing confidently wrong beliefs, with potential applications to AI alignment and robustness.
